headline: Desert Mouse Reveals Protein Linked to Longer, Healthier Lives
meta_description: Scientists discover a protein abundant in long-living desert mice that could unlock secrets to healthy human aging and longevity.
summary_short: Researchers at Yale discovered that golden spiny mice living up to five years in Middle Eastern deserts produce high levels of clusterin, a protein linked to human longe…
body:
While most mice live around nine months in the wild, some golden spiny mice in Middle Eastern deserts survive for up to five years—yet researchers have discovered something even more remarkable than their extended lifespan: these mice remain physically active and mentally sharp throughout their lives.

A team led by Vishwa Deep Dixit, a professor of pathology at Yale School of Medicine, investigated what allows these exceptional rodents to thrive. The answer points to a protein called clusterin, found at unusually high concentrations in immune cells within the mice's fat tissue.

Clusteri is significant because it performs crucial maintenance work: it clears misfolded proteins from the body and reduces inflammation linked to aging-related diseases. In humans, high amounts of clusterin have been identified in centenarians—people aged 100 or older.

When researchers tested clusterin in human white blood cells and laboratory mice, the results were encouraging. The protein lowered inflammation and increased markers associated with healthy aging. In aged mice, it preserved physical activity levels and maintained healthier organ function compared to their peers.

Another discovery: golden spiny mice retain a functional thymus gland—the organ above the heart that produces immune cells—well into old age, while this gland typically deteriorates quickly in other mammals.

Though more research is needed, the findings suggest similar metabolic pathways controlling aging resistance may exist in humans, offering fresh hope for understanding longevity across species.
